summary:
Loss of either BRCA2 or RB1 individually correlates with worse prognosis in prostate cancer due to associations with aggressive features such as high Gleason scores and early progression. Co-loss does not improve prognosis; instead, it remains tied to adverse clinical outcomes despite potential benefits from therapies like PARP inhibition. 

Final Answer Choice:\boxed{B}

Loss of either BRCA2 or RB1 independently predicts poorer prognosis in prostate cancer due to links with aggressive tumor characteristics. There is no evidence that their combined loss leads to improved patient outcomes—even though it confers susceptibility to targeted therapies like PARP inhibitors—which affects treatment but not innate disease behavior.
